definition: A *haruspex* is a diviner who reads the will of the gods in the entrails of a sacrificed animal, above all the liver. Unlike the *augur*, who watches birds, or the *hydromancer*, who reads water, the *haruspex* works from the sacrificial body itself, matching the organ's marks against a schematized map of the divine order.
history: The Latin *haruspex* is a 'gut-gazer' — *haru-* ('entrails') plus a root meaning 'observer.' The Romans, as one Renaissance source recalls, imported from Etruria this class of soothsayer, and the *haruspices* — the diviners who read entrails — remained influential enough that Firmicus Maternus listed them beside priests, augurs, and astrologers among the offices Mercury bestows. Reports of an earthquake sending the *haruspices* to name which god required appeasement show the art woven into the machinery of Roman public religion. Magic revives the office as a class of gut-reading seers.

def_sources.label: Ginzburg, Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath
Bull, The Tradition of Hermes Trismegistus

commentary: The *haruspex* practiced the Etruscan *disciplina*, and reconstructing it is a notorious problem. The Etruscans' own ritual books are lost, and almost everything we know arrives through Roman and later hands that were often hostile, antiquarian, or both. Jean MacIntosh Turfa has worked to recover the system from artifacts — the bronze Piacenza Liver, the Brontoscopic Calendar — while cautioning that Roman writers reshaped Etruscan religion to their own ends. The field now stresses how much of 'Etruscan divination' is a Roman and modern reconstruction of a tradition that left few words of its own.

commentary_sources.label: Jean MacIntosh Turfa, Divining the Etruscan World

etymology.note: Etruscan-derived Roman diviners who read the liver of sacrificed animals against a bronze map of the sky.
etymology.hook: Reading the future in a sheep’s liver.

cards.name: Grim Haruspex
